preoperativeRecommendation:
Physician order required*

Based on POISE-2 Trial:
• Vascular or cardiac surgery: Continue ASA
• Intracranial, spine, or retinal surgery: Hold ASA × 7 days
• Other surgery:
  - No cardiac stents: Hold ASA × 7 days
  - Cardiac stents in situ: Continue ASA*
consultRecommendations:
If stopping ASA, consider consulting:
Cardiology if:
• Within 12 months of elective PCI stenting
• Within 12 months of stenting for ACS (per ESC 2022)

Neurology if:
• Within 3 months of endovascular thrombectomy or carotid stent
actionItems: Confirm physician order received. If not, refer for Anesthesia Review.
additionalClinicalInformation:
Hx of Acute Coronary Syndrome Managed without PCI:
In ACS managed with medical therapy (without PCI), the decision to continue ASA depends on individual risk and benefit. Patients with significant coronary artery disease who haven’t had PCI or surgery due to complex comorbidities may benefit from continuing ASA perioperatively. However, ASA should be stopped for high-risk surgeries (e.g., intracranial, spine, or retinal) where bleeding risk outweighs the benefits.

Hx TAVI: For patients with history of TAVI, ESC 2022 guidelines recommend holding ASA x 7 days if high peri-operative bleeding risk (e.g. undergoing spinal surgery or certain neurosurgical or ophthalmological operations) (27). 

Stroke: For patients with a recent stroke, noncardiac surgery should ideally be delayed for 6 months (37). If surgery is needed sooner, the benefit of continuing ASA is unclear. POISE-2 does not support ASA continuation, as it didn’t prevent perioperative stroke, but data on recent strokes was limited. Some clinicians might recommend ASA based on individual risk factors.

Prostate procedures: Some centers may have local practices of withholding ASA for other procedures such as prostate procedures (3), please refer to local practice pattern guidance in these cases. 

Duration of preoperative Holding: Some experts suggest that ASA can be paused for 3-5 days before surgery, especially for high thrombotic risk patients. Discuss timing and management with the surgeon and internal medicine specialist.

ASSURE trial (2025): This trial suggested there was no benefit to continuing ASA for low/moderate risk non-emergency surgeries if the patient has a modern drug eluting stent that has been in situ for > 12months. (42)

additionalClinicalInformation:
Anticoagulation Management Advice is conservative to allow for neuraxial anesthesia as per the American Society of Regional Anesthesia (4). This may result in a patient withholding their anticoagulation for longer than is absolutely necessary. For patients at high risk of thrombotic complications consider determining if ASRA guidelines need to be followed (e.g. is a spinal, epidural, or nerve blocked planned) and if not a shorter interval off anticoagulation maybe appropriate. Consider referring to the thrombosis canada guidelines (5) This likely requires a multidiscplinary discussion to determine anesthesia needs as well as surgical risk of bleeding. 

Bridging anticoagulation is recommended in patients at high risk of thrombotic complications, please refer to Thrombosis Canada guidelines (5) for bridging recommendation prior to discontinuation. 
Bridging Criteria Summary (5):
High thromboembolic risk (bridging anticoagulation suggested):
- Mechanical prosthetic mitral valve with >1 risk factors (Risk factors include: AF, prior stroke/TIA during anticoagulant interruption or other prior stroke/TIA, prior valve thrombosis, rheumatic heart disease, hypertension, diabetes, congestive heart failure, age ≥75 years.)
- Rheumatic valvular heart disease
- Older generation (cage-ball, tilting disc) mechanical aortic valve
- Chronic atrial fibrillation (valvular or non-valvular) with a CHADS2 score of 5-6
- Recent (within 3 months) arterial thromboembolism (stroke, systemic embolism, transient ischemic attack [TIA])
- Recent (within 3 months) venous thromboembolism (deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism)†
- Prior arterial or venous thromboembolism during appropriate interruption of warfarin
- Severe thrombophilia (deficiency of protein C, protein S or antithrombin; homozygous factor V Leiden or prothrombin gene G20210A mutation or double heterozygous for each mutation; multiple thrombophilias; antiphospholipid antibodies)
- active cancer associated with high VTE risk (pancreatic, primary brain, gastric, esophageal, myeloproliferative)

Intermediate thromboembolic risk (bridging anticoagulation optional and based on individual patient characteristics):
- Chronic atrial fibrillation with a CHADS2 score of 3-4
- Mechanical prosthetic mitral valve without additional risk factors (see above for risk factors)
- Newer generation (bileaflet) mechanical aortic valve with major risk factors for stroke (e.g. multiple prior strokes, prior - --perioperative stroke, prior valve thrombosis)
- Prior venous thromboembolism within last 3-12 months
- Non severe thrombophilia (heterozygous factor V Leiden or prothrombin gene mutation)
- Active cancer or history of cancer

actionItems: Confirm physician order received. If not, refer for Anesthesia Review.
additionalClinicalInformation:
Anticoagulation Management Advice is conservative to allow for neuraxial anesthesia as per the American Society of Regional Anesthesia (4). This may result in a patient withholding their anticoagulation for longer than is absolutely necessary. For patients at high risk of thrombotic complications consider determining if ASRA guidelines need to be followed (e.g. is a spinal, epidural, or nerve blocked planned) and if not a shorter interval off anticoagulation maybe appropriate. Consider referring to the thrombosis canada guidelines (5). This likely requires a multidiscplinary discussion to determine anesthesia needs as well as surgical risk of bleeding. 

Rivaroxaban pharmacology not studied sufficiently to issue recommendations in patients with severe CKD. Speak with expert about holding for longer if GFR < 25 or use thrombosis canada app's perioperative anticoagulation clinical tool for advice on holding.

If appropriate discontinuation interval is not met:
ASRA recommends that a residual plasma level of <30ng/mL or a residual aXa activity plasma level <=0.1IU/mL is acceptable prior to neuraxial block or deep plexus/peripheral block.

additionalClinicalInformation:
Anticoagulation Management Advice is conservative to allow for neuraxial anesthesia as per the American Society of Regional Anesthesia (4). This may result in a patient withholding their anticoagulation for longer than is absolutely necessary. For patients at high risk of thrombotic complications consider determining if ASRA guidelines need to be followed (e.g. is a spinal, epidural, or nerve blocked planned) and if not a shorter interval off anticoagulation maybe appropriate. If appropriate discontinuation interval is not met:
ASRA suggest checking aXa activity, an acceptable level of residual aXa activity remains undetermined, therefore they suggest aXa value of <=0.1IU/mL.

Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia may occur during LMWH administration; ASRA recommends checking platelet count prior to needle placement if patient has been taking LMWH for >4 days. 

ASRA suggests that the presence of blood during needle/catheter placement should delay next dose of LMWH for 24hrs postoperatively.

preoperativeRecommendation: Continue on the day of surgery (DOS)
additionalClinicalInformation:
Trials supporting:
- POISE-3 trial, the hypotension-avoidance and hypertension-avoidance strategies resulted in a similar incidence of major vascular complications. (28) Physicians may consider a patient’s baseline hemodynamics, volume status, and the number of antihypertensive medications, when deciding to continue or hold antihypertensive medications perioperatively.
- STOP or NOT trial showed that continuing ACEi did not result in poor post-op outcomes or increased risk of MACE. It did lead to a slight difference in intra-op hypotension and ACE/ARB increased use of intra-op pressors. (43)

preoperativeRecommendation: Hold on the day of surgery (DOS)*
additionalClinicalInformation: For patients with well-controlled heart failure and stable volume status, we generally recommend holding the morning dose of diuretic on the day of surgery. For patients with heart failure in whom fluid balance has historically been more difficult to control, we recommend continuing the diuretic without interruption. As per ESC 2022 guidelines: retrospective data suggest that intra-operative prescription of diuretics may increase the risk of acute kidney injury after non-cardiac surgery (27)
references:
ESC 2022 Guidelines (Section on Diuretics and Perioperative Volume Management):
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36017553/

preoperativeRecommendation: Hold 3 days preoperatively*
additionalClinicalInformation:
Holding for 72hrs preoperatively is to prevent the development of perioperative euglycemic ketoacidosis. 
Euglycemic ketoacidosis is a rare but potentially life threatening complication associated with SGLT-2 inhibitors. This condition is characterized by the development of ketoacidosis in the presence of normal or near-normal blood glucose levels. It poses a particular risk perioperatively due to factors such as surgical stress, dehydration, and reduced food intake, which can exacerbate the risk of ketoacidosis. (31, 36)
references:
FDA Drug Safety Communication - SGLT2 Inhibitors and Ketoacidosis:
https://www.fda.gov/drugs/drug-safety-and-availability/fda-drug-safety-communication-fda-warns-about-rare-occurrences-serious-infection-around-heart

Perioperative SGLT2 Inhibitor Management - Cleveland Clinic J Med (2025):
https://www.ccjm.org/content/92/7/429

Preoperative SGLT2i Use and Postoperative DKA (JAMA Surg 2025):
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39969891/

preoperativeRecommendation: Continue on the day of surgery (DOS) + Advise modified preoperative fasting
additionalClinicalInformation:
Liraglutide Half Life= 13h
Dosing Interval = Once Daily

Evidence on perioperative management of GLP-1 receptor agonists is evolving, and recommendations differ across groups (32, 33, 34, 35). Most national and international multisociety guidance supports continuing GLP-1 RAs. A recent review (44) proposes a modified preoperative fast. 
Locally, Vancouver General Hospital and Island Health recommend a 24hr period of clear fluids only before surgery such as:
- Light breakfast before 8 AM the day before surgery
- Clear fluids only thereafter
- NPO at least 4 h before surgery

For patients with gastrointestinal symptoms (abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) or recent dose escalation, advise frequent small bland meals before starting the clear fluid fast, and pause further dose escalation until the patient is tolerating diet after surgery if feesible.
references:
Multisociety Consensus Recommendations for Perioperative Management of GLP-1 RAs (Anesthesiology 2024):
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38889366/

ASA Practice Advisory on GLP-1 Receptor Agonists (2023):
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37390355/

preoperativeRecommendation:
Physician Order Required

Continue on the day of surgery (DOS)*
consultRecommendations:
Consider consulting addictions medicine service or RACE line. 
Consider liaising with community prescriber to re-titrate back to usual dose postoperatively if altered perioperatively.
additionalClinicalInformation:
Suboxone, combining buprenorphine and naloxone, greatly reduces the risk of overdose deaths and is effective for managing opioid addiction. The number needed to treat (NNT) to prevent one overdose death is approximately 3 to 4, highlighting its critical role in reducing mortality. Continuing Suboxone during surgery can prevent withdrawal symptoms and support addiction treatment, but its partial opioid agonist effect might affect postoperative pain management. When deciding whether to continue Suboxone, it is important to balance the benefits of addiction management with potential effects on pain relief. Available evidence indicates that some mu-opioid receptors remain available for binding by full mu agonists, allowing effective analgesia even when buprenorphine is used alongside these agents. A multimodal analgesic approach, incorporating adjunctive medications and regional anesthesia, is recommended for optimal postoperative pain control. (41)

Pharmacology:
- Dosing: PO once daily
- Medication half life = 37hrs

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32168380/

additionalClinicalInformation:
Sublocade, a long-acting injectable form of buprenorphine. The number needed to treat (NNT) to prevent one overdose death is approximately 3 to 4, highlighting its critical role in reducing mortality. It is used for opioid addiction treatment and can help manage withdrawal symptoms. Continuing Sublocade during surgery supports addiction management but may influence postoperative pain control due to its partial opioid agonist effect. When deciding whether to continue Sublocade, balance the benefits of preventing relapse and managing addiction against potential impacts on pain management. Available evidence indicates that some mu-opioid receptors remain available for binding by full mu agonists, allowing effective analgesia even when buprenorphine is used alongside these agents. A multimodal analgesic approach, incorporating adjunctive medications and regional anesthesia, is recommended for optimal postoperative pain control. (41)

Pharmacology:
- Dosing: Monthly SQ
- Medication Half Life = 43-60 days (therapeutic levels remain for 2-5 months depending on dose (100 or 300mg respectively)

additionalClinicalInformation:
Butrans, a transdermal patch containing buprenorphine, is commonly used for chronic pain management. Continuing Butrans during surgery can help maintain pain control and prevent withdrawal symptoms. Although buprenorphine is a partial opioid agonist, available evidence indicates that some mu-opioid receptors remain available for binding by full mu agonists, allowing effective analgesia even when used alongside these agents for postoperative pain management. A multimodal analgesic approach, incorporating adjunctive medications and regional anesthesia, is recommended to optimize postoperative pain control. (41)

additionalClinicalInformation:
For elective surgery where opioids may be required, hold oral naltrexone for ≥72 h preoperatively. Resume only once opioids are no longer needed. 50 mg = 24 hours 
100 mg = 48 hours 
150 mg = 72 hours 

t1/2 = 4 hours; active metabolite t1/2= 13 hours

preoperativeRecommendation: Medication can be continued if interacting medications are avoided perioperatively. (29)
actionItems: 1. Slate Flagging Consideration: Mark as MAO-I on slate
additionalClinicalInformation:
MAO-i Safe Anesthesia: (29)

Opioids with serotonin toxicity risk with MAO-I: meperidine, tramadol, methadone, tapentadol, dextromethorphan, pentazocine

Safe opioid alternatives: oxycodone, fentanyl, morphine, codeine, buprenorphine
   
Vasopressors that are contraindicated with MAO-I: ephedrine, pseudoephedrine, metaraminol, IV dopamine, topical cocaine

Safe alternatives when titrated to effect (consider lower initial dose): epinephrine, norepinephrine, phenylephrine, isoproterenol, midodrine, dobutamine

Methylene blue must be avoided with MAO-I.
references:
Perioperative Management of Parkinson's Disease (J Neurosurg Anesthesiol 2020):
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32740119/

preoperativeRecommendation: Medication can be continued if interacting medications are avoided perioperatively. (29)
actionItems: 1. Slate Flagging Consideration: Mark as MAO-I on slate
additionalClinicalInformation:
MAO-i Safe Anesthesia: (29)

Opioids with serotonin toxicity risk with MAO-I: meperidine, tramadol, methadone, tapentadol, dextromethorphan, pentazocine

Safe opioid alternatives: oxycodone, fentanyl, morphine, codeine, buprenorphine
   
Vasopressors that are contraindicated with MAO-I: ephedrine, pseudoephedrine, metaraminol, IV dopamine, topical cocaine

Safe alternatives when titrated to effect (consider lower initial dose): epinephrine, norepinephrine, phenylephrine, isoproterenol, midodrine, dobutamine

Methylene blue must be avoided with MAO-I.

additionalClinicalInformation:

Suggest restart after adequate wound healing has occured.
